---
title: "Tactics That Matter"
description: "Flanking, morale and routing, the unit triangle, terrain, and disengaging."
---

Numbers aren't everything. Sparta's battles are decided by **where** units are and
**which way they face** --- flanking, morale, and the rock-paper-scissors of unit
types turn an even fight lopsided.

## Flanking

Hitting a unit from the side or rear deals far more damage *and* morale loss than a
frontal clash:

::: {#tbl-flank-multipliers}

| Attack angle | Multiplier |
| --- | --- |
| Front | ×1.0 |
| Side (flank) | **×1.5** |
| Rear | **×2.0** |

Damage and morale multipliers by attack angle.
:::

**Maneuver behind the enemy line.** A regiment pinned frontally by a screen while
your cavalry hits its rear will melt --- and start a rout that spreads.

**The enemy watches its own flanks too.** An AI-controlled unit that finds a live
threat closing on its side or rear turns to meet it --- it won't stand fighting
head-on while your cavalry lines up an unanswered flank strike. Catching the enemy
still costs real setup: bring the second threat into contact before it reacts, or
pin the front so the flanking unit has nowhere better to turn.

## Morale & routing

Units don't fight to the last soldier. When a unit loses enough soldiers or takes
flank/rear hits, it **routs** --- it flees (its flag fading) toward its own back edge
and stops fighting for now. A routing unit can flee past the field's visible edge into a
margin beyond it before it's gone for good, staying on the field --- and still
fightable --- for as long as it's plausibly still in view.

- **Routs are contagious.** A nearby ally watching a unit break is more likely to
  break too. One collapse can cascade down a line.
- This makes morale a *weapon*: you can win by breaking the enemy's will before
  you've killed even half their soldiers.
- **A routing unit is still fair game.** Routing doesn't grant immunity --- a fleeing
  unit can still be struck and run down, chased as relentlessly as your pursuer can
  manage. So chase a broken enemy down to finish it, rather than assuming it's out of
  the fight.
- **A "broken" unit can rally** if nothing catches it. As it flees, its nerve steadies
  --- morale climbs back toward a baseline, fast at first and levelling off. If it
  **breaks contact** (no enemy close enough to keep hitting it) and still fields enough
  men, its recovering morale carries it over the rally threshold and it **returns to
  your control**, ready to fight again.
- **Keep pressing it and it "shatters" instead** --- still in contact when its time
  runs out, or gutted past reforming. A shattered unit does NOT vanish: it stays on the
  field, still routing, but can never again recover morale or rally. From there it
  either **escapes** (flees clear of the map, gone but got away) or is run down and
  **annihilated** (soldiers reduced to zero) before it can.
- **A routing unit paths around impassable terrain too** --- fleeing is no longer a
  straight line toward the back edge; a router detours around a hill in its way the
  same as an ordered move does. If every escape direction is blocked, it gives up
  fleeing and stands to fight instead of uselessly running in place against the
  obstacle.
- **The battle isn't over while a side is only routing.** A team is beaten only once
  its last unit is truly gone (escaped, annihilated, or otherwise removed), not while
  its final unit is merely routing. Even a lone survivor that breaks can flee, rally,
  and fight on --- so press a rout home rather than assuming a broken last unit is a win.

::: {#fig-morale-recovery .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/morale_recovery.jpg">
  <source src="media/morale_recovery.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

A broken unit recovering with nothing to interfere: staged already routing, with no
enemy anywhere on the field, so pursuit and further casualties can't factor in. Its
nerve steadies as it flees, morale climbs back over the rally threshold, and it
returns to your control. (The regression guard for this behavior is
`test_morale_recovery_demo_scenario.gd`.)
:::

::: {#fig-rout-rally .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/rout_rally.jpg">
  <source src="media/rout_rally.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Rout and pursuit: a low-morale infantry block is charged by cavalry and breaks &mdash;
it flees, its flag fading, toward its own edge. The faster cavalry catch it before it can put
real distance between them, and the casualties from being run down keep its morale
too low to ever recover: it's annihilated rather than rallying &mdash; contrast the
isolated recovery above (@fig-morale-recovery), where nothing interferes.
:::

::: {#fig-last-unit-rally .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/last_unit_rally.jpg">
  <source src="media/last_unit_rally.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

A team's last unit routs. A lone player infantry is broken and flees, its flag fading;
the enemy keeps pressing since a routing unit is still fair game, so this
matchup grinds on rather than resolving cleanly &mdash; but the point still
holds: the battle stays live instead of ending the instant the last unit
broke, for as long as it's still on the field.
:::

::: {#fig-trapped-routing .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/trapped_routing.jpg">
  <source src="media/trapped_routing.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Routing around terrain: a weak unit breaks and flees straight toward the hill,
but paths around the obstacle instead of walking into it, the same way an
ordered march does. Two pursuing cavalry keep the pressure on throughout,
gradually grinding it down instead of letting it put real distance between
them. A router with no terrain escape route left at all stands and fights
instead of fleeing in place (see [Terrain](#terrain)) &mdash; the open field
this clip runs in never actually boxes a unit in, so that specific case isn't
shown here.
:::

## Rank cycling (trained units)

A **well-trained** melee unit periodically rotates its ranks --- the tired front
line steps back and a fresh rank takes its place, signalled by a whistle. Cycling
**slows fatigue buildup** (by up to half at full training) and lets the unit
**recover morale even while still fighting**. The better trained the unit, the more
often it cycles. Ranged units don't cycle.

Fatigue builds at a realistic pace --- sustained melee wears a unit down over
**minutes**, not seconds, and resting in the rear restores it just as gradually.
So relief is a deliberate decision for a prolonged fight, not a frantic
every-few-seconds chore.

Training therefore pays off in a long grind: a drilled regiment wears down a green
one of equal numbers, because the green unit tires and wavers first. Rank cycling
buys TIME, not immunity: the recovery it grants fades as the regiment thins out
(fewer fresh files left to rotate to the front) and stops once it's badly
bloodied. So a veteran unit facing the same sustained pressure still routs
eventually --- it just takes noticeably longer and costs the attacker more losses
to break it than a green unit under the same attack.

**Rank relief (discipline toggle).** Rank cycling is on by default for every unit,
but you can switch it off with <kbd>I</kbd> (see [How to play](how-to-play.qmd) for
the control). A unit with rank relief off holds its ranks fixed --- no fatigue
relief, no in-fight morale recovery from cycling --- which trades away that
endurance edge for a line that never opens the small gaps a whistled rotation
briefly creates. Leave it on for a grinding attritional fight; consider switching
it off only when you'd rather keep the front rank locked in place than let it
step back mid-melee.

## Line relief

Rank cycling rotates soldiers *within* a regiment; **line relief** rotates whole
regiments. Right-click an engaged friendly with a fresh unit selected: the fresh
unit advances through its tired ally and takes over the melee, and the relieved
regiment falls back toward its own rear --- where fatigue drains away and morale
recovers (see [How to play](how-to-play.qmd) for the controls).

- **The swap is a pass-through.** For its duration the two friendly regiments may
  interpenetrate, so the line never opens a gap for the enemy to pour into while
  the fresh unit works its way forward.
- **The retreat is an order like any other.** The relieved unit falls back under a
  normal move order (hold <kbd>Space</kbd> to see it), and you can redirect it ---
  to a safer rest spot, or straight to another fight if you must.
- **Interrupting the reliever cancels the swap.** The swap lives on the reliever's
  relief order, so a fresh order mid-pass ends it where it stands and the pair
  stops ignoring each other's bodies. Avoid changing your mind while the two
  regiments still overlap.
- **Expect to bleed a little on the way out.** The enemy keeps swinging while the
  retiring ranks are in reach, so pulling out of contact costs a few men --- far
  fewer than staying until the regiment breaks.

Rotate before the break, not after: a tired regiment fights measurably worse (see
[Stamina](#stamina)) and routs sooner, and pulling it out while it still holds
banks a rested unit for the next push.

## Passage of lines

Line relief swaps one pair of regiments at a time by default --- every fresh unit
you order onto an engaged friendly swaps with (or, past the first, piles onto the
fight of) that ONE unit you clicked. When a whole tired line needs relieving at
once, select every fresh unit in your reserve, switch the group-order mode to
**Distributed** (<kbd>X</kbd>, or the **Spread** button --- see
[How to play](how-to-play.qmd) for the control), then right-click any one engaged
unit in the tired line. Each fresh unit pairs with its OWN nearby engaged
friendly instead of all of them swapping with the single unit clicked, so the
whole reserve passes through the whole tired line in one order --- Polybius's
triplex acies, a fresh line relieving a spent one through gaps opened in the
line behind it.

- **The candidate pool is engaged friendlies only.** A reliever never swaps with
  an idle or already-routing unit just to keep busy --- if fewer of your tired
  line are actually fighting than you have fresh units, the extras fall back to
  piling onto a fight instead of manufacturing a swap.
- **Pairing is sorted by proximity to the unit you clicked, not to each
  reliever's own position.** With reserves lined up abreast of a tired line,
  this usually pairs each fresh unit with roughly the ally in front of it, but
  it can cross --- the same distribution rule distributed attack already uses
  for spreading a multi-unit attack across several enemies.
- **Every pairing is a full line relief**, with the same pass-through
  exemption, the same real retreat order, and the same "expect to bleed a
  little on the way out" cost described above --- distributed relief only
  changes how the targets are assigned, not how any one swap behaves.

## Acies triplex

The Roman **acies triplex** deployed infantry in three lines --- hastati,
principes, triarii --- with gaps between maniples in one line roughly
matching their own frontage width, and the line behind offset to cover those
gaps: a checkerboard (quincunx) pattern. Passage of lines (above) builds the
*tactical* half --- swapping a whole tired line for a fresh one through gaps.
**Checkerboard form-up** builds the *spatial* half: a fifth multi-unit
form-up split mode (off by default; enable it in **☰ Menu → Form-up ---
Checkerboard (quincunx)**, or cycle to it with <kbd>Y</kbd> once enabled) that
lays your selection out as two staggered rows instead of one straight line.
See [How to play: Controls](how-to-play.qmd#controls) (the "Deploy several
units at once" entry) for the drag mechanics.

This is a **one-shot layout**, not a standing formation --- a unit dragged
into a checkerboard arrangement doesn't remember "I'm line 2" the way it
remembers its stance or formation mode. Nothing yet models the historical
system's chief weakness either: a gapped, multi-line formation trades
frontage flexibility for flank exposure, so a checkerboard-formed army isn't
currently more vulnerable to flanking than a solid line. See
`docs/acies-triplex-design.md` in the project repository for the full design
note and what's tracked as follow-up work.

## Echelon (oblique order)

Every form-up split covered so far keeps the whole selection on one straight
rank. **Echelon** breaks that on purpose: it staggers your line into a
diagonal, one flank stepped forward and the other held back. Caesar deployed
this way at times in the Gallic War, and Frederick the Great refined it into
his signature **oblique order** at Leuthen --- weight one flank, refuse the
other, and let the enemy fight an uneven battle instead of a fair one.

Two more multi-unit form-up split modes carry it, both off by default (enable
one in **☰ Menu → Form-up**, or cycle to it with <kbd>Y</kbd> once enabled):

- **Echelon (right leads)** --- the unit nearest your drag's right flank
  advances furthest, the left-flank unit trails furthest back, and everyone
  in between steps evenly across the gap. Concentrate the leading (right)
  flank on a weak point in the enemy line while the trailing (left) flank
  stays out of easy reach.
- **Echelon (left leads)** --- the mirror image: the left flank leads, the
  right flank refuses.

See [How to play: Controls](how-to-play.qmd#controls) (the "Deploy several
units at once" entry) for the drag mechanics --- echelon reuses the exact
same box-select-then-drag gesture every other split mode does, just with a
staggered depth instead of one flat line.

Like checkerboard, this is a **one-shot layout, not a standing formation**: a
unit dragged into an echelon doesn't remember which flank it led from, so a
later reform or reshape doesn't preserve the stagger. And nothing currently
models the tactical risk a real refused flank carries either --- the
trailing units aren't rendered any more exposed to being turned than a
straight line would be, so the payoff (concentrating force where you choose)
isn't yet balanced by the historical downside (a flank that isn't in the
fight yet is a flank an enemy could exploit before it arrives).

## Stamina

Every soldier carries a **stamina pool** that drains through combat and refills at rest.
Each blow thrown costs stamina; throwing blows costs more than absorbing them. Rising from the ground after
being knocked down costs a burst extra. Low stamina lowers the soldier's **condition
factor**, which scales both attack output and defence --- a tired soldier hits softer and
is easier to hit.

So a unit that has been grinding at the front for a while is measurably weaker than one
that just arrived fresh. Rank cycling (above) helps by rotating tired soldiers to the
rear to recover; simply pulling a unit out of melee and letting it rest works too.

## Prone / knockdown

A blow that delivers enough force can **knock a soldier off their feet**. A prone
soldier has no active defence --- only their armour absorbs incoming hits --- and must spend
both time (about two seconds) and stamina to get back up.

Visually, prone soldiers stand out:

- **At normal zoom (mark LOD):** a prone soldier's mark is flattened into a wide
  horizontal sliver so it reads clearly as "lying flat" among the standing marks.
- **Zoomed in (figure LOD):** the figure silhouette is rotated 90° to show a person
  lying on their side.

**Engaged lines resist knockdown.** A unit that is actively locked in melee (and not
in Skirmish stance) braces automatically, raising the impulse threshold needed to knock
a soldier flat. A skirmishing or unengaged unit does not brace and is easier to topple.
A deeper file also helps --- more soldiers absorbing the same front-rank pressure means
fewer individual soldiers pushed past the knockdown threshold.

A cluster of prone soldiers in the front rank is a warning sign: your line is tired or
outmatched there, and each fallen body briefly opens a gap in your defence before they
can rise.

## Line width (frontage)

You set how wide each regiment fights --- <kbd>[</kbd> and <kbd>]</kbd> step the
width by a file symmetrically, or drag a selected unit's flank grips (the drag
anchors the far flank, so the line extends toward the grip you pull --- handy for
stretching a line toward the flank you need to cover without giving ground on the
other side). The same soldiers spread across a different shape:

- **Wide and shallow** puts more men in the front rank, so more of them are
  swinging at once --- good for overlapping an enemy's flank or covering ground so a
  foe can't slip around your line.
- **Narrow and deep** stacks more ranks behind a thinner front. Fewer fight at any
  moment, but the column has more bodies to feed forward as the front falls --- it
  holds a chokepoint or punches through one spot rather than spreading thin.

A wider line is easier to outlast in depth and quicker to crumble once it bends;
a deeper one trades frontage for staying power. A width you've set by hand (the
bracket keys or a flank drag) holds exactly as you left it --- the game never
overrides your explicit choice.

**Close the ranks.** Left on auto, though, a regiment that's lost half its strength
narrows its own frontage one notch, trading width for depth so the survivors reform
into a smaller, still-solid block rather than stretching a nearly-spent line across
its original width. It widens back out again if the regiment claws back above about
two-thirds strength (reinforcement, a relief, a lull in the fighting) --- but not the
instant it edges past halfway, so a unit trading casualties right around the line
doesn't flicker between shapes fight to fight.

**File-doubling for a fast reshape.** The <kbd>[</kbd> / <kbd>]</kbd> steps are fine for
fine-tuning, but two drill maneuvers swap the whole shape in one press. <kbd>B</kbd>
(**explicatio**) doubles the frontage and halves the depth --- a column unfolds into a wide
line to overlap a flank or plug a gap. <kbd>N</kbd> (**duplicatio**) does the reverse: it
halves the frontage and doubles the depth, folding a broad line into a deep column so you can
squeeze down a lane or mass depth to punch through one point. The men walk to their new slots
rather than snapping, so there's a beat of disorder while the block reshapes --- don't start
the maneuver in contact, and give it room before the enemy arrives. A natural rhythm is
duplicatio to march a column through a chokepoint, then explicatio to deploy back into line on
the far side.

**Anchored explicatio for a pinned flank.** The plain explicatio widens about the unit's own
centre, so both flanks drift when the frontage doubles --- fine in the open, but a problem when
one flank is braced against a hill, a river, or a neighbouring unit that can't shift with it.
<kbd>Shift+B</kbd> widens while holding the right flank fixed; <kbd>Ctrl+B</kbd> holds the left.
The whole widen lands on the free flank instead, so the pinned side never moves and the line
still gets the extra frontage where there's room for it.

**How a line reforms after losses.** The front rank takes the hits, but the block loses
depth from the rear. The front rank keeps its full width, the file behind each fighter feeds
men forward to hold the contact line, and the depth shrinks rank by rank. When the rearmost rank is left
short-handed, its survivors close up toward the centre --- the wings shorten first while
the middle files stay deepest --- rather than leaving a ragged, gap-toothed back rank. So a
mauled unit still presents a solid front and never bulges wider than the frontage you set;
you read its losses as a shrinking depth, not a fraying line.

**Deploy a whole battle line at once.** Select several regiments and drag one flank
line (right-click and drag): the line splits among them. By default it splits to **equal
depth (space)**, so every regiment forms up to the same physical depth --- a uniform front
where bigger (or denser) units just take a wider slice. That keeps your whole line's staying
power even, with no thin spot for the enemy to punch through. Press <kbd>Y</kbd> (or
<kbd>Shift</kbd> + <kbd>Y</kbd> to cycle the other way) to switch modes --- **equal width**
when you'd rather every unit cover the same frontage (small units stack deeper), or one of
the count-based variants when you want density differences ignored entirely (see
**Deploy several units at once** in [How to Play](how-to-play.qmd) for what each of the four
modes holds equal). They sort left-to-right by where they already stand, so nobody crosses
the field to reach a slot --- a fast way to dress a ragged group into one clean front before
the enemy closes. Hold Shift to place them in selection order instead when you want a
specific regiment on a specific flank.

Use **☰ Menu → Form-up** to configure the split: the upper radio buttons set the mode
each battle starts in; the lower checkboxes ("Y-key cycles through…") control which modes
<kbd>Y</kbd> steps between mid-battle. Uncheck a mode to skip it entirely from the cycle.

## The unit triangle (rock-paper-scissors)

Three roles, each strong against one and weak to another:

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### 🗡️ Infantry
The reliable line. No special bonus --- your anvil to pin the enemy in place while
something else does the killing.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### 🐎 Cavalry
**Fast** --- they charge at a hard canter (~8.5 m/s, far quicker than any foot
unit's advance) --- with a **charge bonus** that grows with closing speed.
Devastating into a flank or rear, but blunted when it charges into braced spears.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### 🛡️ Spearmen
Anti-cavalry (marked with a spear). They **blunt the cavalry charge**, so use them
to **screen** your line against enemy horse. Their long spears also **out-reach**
shorter weapons, landing the first blows as lines close (see below).
:::

:::

**The loop:** cavalry beats exposed flanks → spearmen beat cavalry → infantry pin so
your cavalry can get *around* to those flanks. Read the enemy's screen before you
commit your horse.

## Formation stances

Press <kbd>T</kbd> to cycle a unit's density --- **Normal**, **Tight** (closed ranks:
better missile and charge defence), **Loose** (open order: room to fire), and **Square**
(the anti-cavalry ring, covered below). <kbd>Shift</kbd> + <kbd>T</kbd> cycles the other
way, handy for stepping back one density without looping all the way round. Two heavier
**shielded close-order stances**
build on Tight's locked-shield density and are set from the control-bar Formation menu or
their own direct keys.

::: {#fig-formation-shift-reverse .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/formation_shift_reverse.jpg">
  <source src="media/formation_shift_reverse.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Formation density cycling with the Shift-reverse: <kbd>T</kbd> steps Tight to
Loose, then <kbd>Shift</kbd> + <kbd>T</kbd> steps back to Tight --- the same
parallelism as Shift+Y and Shift+X reversing the form-up-split and
group-attack-mode cycles.
:::

- **Shield Wall** (<kbd>L</kbd>) --- shields locked edge-to-edge, a static line. It
  sharply cuts the missile and melee damage a unit takes **from the front**, but only
  from the front: a shot or blow into the flank or rear ignores the wall. The wall barely
  moves, so it is a **holding** stance. Anchor a flank or a chokepoint with it and let the
  enemy break on the front --- but keep its flanks screened, because that is where it is soft.
- **Testudo** (<kbd>U</kbd>) --- shields locked front, sides, and overhead: the strongest
  missile cover in the game, and it works **from every direction**. The cost is that the
  men fight weakly (packed head-down under their shields) and move slowest of all. It is an
  **arrow-crossing** stance, not a fighting one: walk a unit through a beaten zone of
  archery in testudo, then drop to Tight or Normal before it reaches melee.

Both shielded stances still **brace against a cavalry charge** the way Tight does, so
neither is helpless if horse reaches them --- but only spearmen actually turn a charge
back. Because both are slow, commit them deliberately: a testudo caught in the open by
melee, or a shield wall taken in the flank, is in trouble.

::: {#fig-testudo-under-fire .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/testudo_under_fire.jpg">
  <source src="media/testudo_under_fire.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Testudo under fire: an infantry unit locked in testudo, ringed by archers firing
from all four sides, weathers the volleys that its overhead shields blunt from
every direction.
:::

## Form square against cavalry

When horse threatens a unit's flank or rear and you have no spear screen to spare,
form a hollow **square** at once: <kbd>O</kbd> (for *orbis*) jumps straight to the
Square stance, and <kbd>T</kbd> also reaches it by cycling through Tight and Loose. The
soldiers face outward on every side, so the unit presents **no weak facing**: an
attack from any direction lands as if it were frontal (the usual ×1.5 flank / ×2 rear
bonus is gone), and a cavalry charge into the ring is braced and **backfires** whether
it hits the front, a flank, or the rear. There is no open side for the horse to
exploit. The soldiers' own outward-facing marks around the block's perimeter make the
stance easy to spot on the field --- no separate ring overlay drawn on top.

The brace is physical, not a flat damage penalty. Watch a charging cavalry regiment
hit a set square: its riders' momentum is genuinely absorbed by the individual
bodies it collides with, not waved through by a rule, so the charge visibly slows
and settles at the ring instead of riding on through it.

Square is a defensive commitment, not a free upgrade. While it holds, the unit
**crawls** (it can't charge out) and **hits softer** (reduced offensive output). So:

**The AI squares up on its own.** An AI-controlled spear unit forms Square the
moment enemy cavalry closes on it, with no player prompting it --- the same
reaction a disciplined human commander would take. Charging AI spearmen in the
open only works if the cavalry arrives before the square finishes forming.

- **Square up early** --- before the charge lands, not as it connects. The mobility
  penalty means a late square may not finish forming in time.
- **Drop back to Normal** the moment the horse is beaten off or pulls away, or the
  offence and mobility penalties will cost you against enemy foot.
- **It's a fallback, not a replacement for spearmen.** A spear screen still blunts the
  charge *and* keeps your line mobile and hitting hard; square is what you reach for
  when a unit is caught in the open with cavalry on a bad angle.

**Orbis vs schiltron.** The hollow square comes in two variants with different
trade-offs, not one generic stance:

- **Orbis** (<kbd>O</kbd>, or the control bar's **Square (Orbis)** button) is the
  last-stand ring. Its charge brace and offence sit at the square's baseline, but it
  holds its nerve better than any other stance --- casualties erode its morale more
  slowly, so a surrounded unit fighting to the end is less likely to break outright.
  Reach for it when a unit is cut off and simply needs to survive as long as possible.
- **Schiltron** (<kbd>Shift</kbd> + <kbd>O</kbd>, or the control bar's **Schiltron**
  button) is the cavalry specialist. Its denser hedge of levelled spears braces a
  charge *harder* than the orbis does --- cavalry riding into a schiltron comes off
  worse than riding into an orbis --- at the cost of hitting even softer in return,
  and it carries no morale bonus. Reach for it when the priority is breaking the
  charge itself, not outlasting
  a siege.

Both variants share the same hollow-square footprint and the same mobility penalty;
only the charge-bracing strength, offence, and morale resilience differ.

::: {#fig-square .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/square.jpg">
  <source src="media/square.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Anti-cavalry square: an infantry unit is charged in the rear by two cavalry,
then cycled to Square (T). The hollow ring faces outward on every side,
so the rear charge finds no weak facing and is braced from behind &mdash;
the unit holds where a rear-charged line would break.
:::

::: {#fig-schiltron .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/schiltron.jpg">
  <source src="media/schiltron.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Schiltron: the same rear cavalry charge as above, but the unit forms
Schiltron (Shift+O) instead of Orbis. The denser hedge braces the charge
harder than orbis does, at a deeper offence cost.
:::

::: {#fig-battle-ai-leaders .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/battle_ai_leaders.jpg">
  <source src="media/battle_ai_leaders.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Two AI unit-leader decisions in one clash: the AI spearmen on the left form the
anti-cavalry square on their own as player cavalry closes, with no player key
press arming it. The AI infantry on the right advances and attacks, then
re-targets and turns to face a second player unit that closes on its flank
instead of fighting on with its back exposed.
:::

## Enemy coordination

The enemy's regiments don't fight in isolation --- a subcommander watches the
whole group and hands each unit leader a standing directive on top of its own
battlefield instincts. Three behaviours come out of this:

- **Line integrity** --- a unit that races far ahead of its neighbors gets
  held back near the group's own depth instead of closing on the enemy alone.
- **Mutual support** --- an unengaged neighbor gets sent to guard an ally
  that's already fighting, rather than standing idle nearby.
- **Flank coverage** --- a unit repositions to cover a group flank an enemy is
  bearing down on, before casualties or a maneuver leave it open.

A directive never overrides a unit's own immediate reactions --- turning to
face a flank threat, forming anti-cavalry square, and calling for relief all
still come first. It only shapes what an otherwise-idle unit does next, so
the enemy line holds together without losing the individual judgement it
already had.

::: {#fig-subcommander-mutual-support .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/subcommander_mutual_support.jpg">
  <source src="media/subcommander_mutual_support.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Mutual support: one enemy unit stays locked in a long fight while its
neighbor's own foe dies fast, freeing it up. The subcommander sends the
freed unit to guard its still-fighting neighbor, and it closes the distance
to join the melee directly.
:::

## The general

Above the subcommander sits a general, driven by a **doctrine profile** ---
data, not code, so a non-programmer can author a new one. Each profile sets:

- **Which army plans it knows** --- an advance-in-line or an envelop split
  into two wings, picked each AI tick against how the battle actually looks
  (an envelop needs a real numeric edge over the enemy, not just an
  even fight).
- **A reserve fraction** --- how many units start held back instead of
  joining the active line.
- **A reserve-commit threshold** --- once the active line's own
  weighted-average morale drops below this, the reserves fold in.
- **Whether to pursue routers** --- an aggressive doctrine chases a broken
  enemy down; a cautious one holds the line instead.

Two doctrines reading the identical battle can commit to different plans.
A held-back reserve doesn't just sit inert, either --- it still turns to
face a flank threat, forms anti-cavalry square, or answers a nearby ally's
call for relief, exactly like any other unit; only its own advance is put
on hold.

::: {#fig-general-doctrine-reserves .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/general_doctrine_reserves.jpg">
  <source src="media/general_doctrine_reserves.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

A cautious general holds two full-strength units in reserve while three
weaker units alone meet the enemy. As the front line takes losses its
morale crosses the doctrine's threshold, and the general folds the
reserves into the fight --- they break their stationary hold, march up,
and join the melee.
:::

## Weapon reach

Each weapon has an **effective reach** --- how close two units must get before they
trade blows. A longer weapon reaches that contact distance first, so it lands the
opening strikes of a clash while a shorter-weapon enemy is still closing the gap:

- **Spears** reach furthest (~2.4 m) --- spearmen strike first as the lines meet, on
  top of blunting cavalry charges.
- **Swords** (infantry, cavalry) are the mid-length baseline (~1.3--1.5 m).
- **Archers** carry only a short sidearm (~0.6 m); they're meant to shoot, not duel
  in the press.

So a spear line meeting a sword line head-on gets free hits during the approach ---
another reason to lead with spearmen and let the shorter weapons pin and flank. Combat
now resolves **per soldier** by weapon reach: a spearman strikes any enemy within its
longer reach, while a swordsman beyond its own (shorter) reach cannot strike back. The
*sustained* standoff --- a spear line physically holding swordsmen out at weapon length
--- now emerges from **knockback**: a landed blow shoves the struck soldier back, so a
spear line keeps knocking swordsmen out past their own reach while staying in its. The
lines no longer snap apart by a spacing rule; the gap is the live balance of the press
forward against the knockback, so it breathes as the fight ebbs and flows.

**Knockback now pushes both ways.** Every blow obeys Newton's third law: the struck
soldier is shoved back as before, but the striking soldier also recoils along the same
line, split between the two by effective mass (bracing counts as extra mass). A light,
unbraced attacker rocks backward hard on contact; a heavy or set formation barely
moves. That's why a cavalry charge into a braced spear wall now visibly backfires on
the horses themselves, not just on the spearmen holding their ground --- the heavier,
set defenders absorb most of the shove and hand a proportionally bigger kick back to
the lighter attacker.

## Movement speed

Units move at **realistic real-world speeds**, measured in metres per second, with
**three independent paces** per type --- walk, jog, and sprint (see
[Pace modes](#pace-modes) below) --- scaled to how much each type's panoply weighs:

::: {#tbl-unit-speeds}

| Type | Walk | Jog | Sprint |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Spearmen (heaviest kit) | 1.1 m/s | 1.8 m/s | 2.8 m/s |
| Infantry | 1.3 m/s | 2.5 m/s | 4.0 m/s |
| Archers (lightest kit) | 1.5 m/s | 3.0 m/s | 4.5 m/s |
| Cavalry | 1.7 m/s (walk gait) | 3.5 m/s (trot) | 8.5 m/s (hard canter) |

Movement paces by unit type, in metres per second.
:::

Heavier armor and weapons cost proportionally more at a run than at a walk --- walking
pace barely changes under load, but running form degrades fast, so spearmen's walk
sits close to the lighter types' while their sprint lags well behind. Cavalry's walk
and jog map onto the horse's own real walk/trot gaits rather than a rider-weight-scaled
fraction, since a rider's kit is a small fraction of what the horse itself carries.
Its charge pace is deliberately toned down from a true historical gallop (which would
close distance faster than the map/camera scale reads well) toward a fast canter ---
see `docs/SPEED_VERIFICATION.md` for the historical comparison.

That sprint gap is a lever: cavalry cross the field and reach an exposed flank far
sooner than infantry can re-form to face them, so commit horse decisively and time
your foot to be where the charge lands. (The faster the gallop at the moment of
contact, the bigger the charge bonus --- so a clean run-up hits harder than a
standing start.)

## Pace modes

By default, foot units advance on **AUTO** pace: they walk on the approach, jog
when a ranged enemy is firing at them (to spend less time under missiles), and
sprint at full speed once within about 200 px of the target --- so a charge
arrives at full momentum without wearing out the troops on the march.

**Toggle Walk advance (☰ Menu → Walk advance)** to lock units to walk pace
throughout. This is required for formed stances like shield wall or pike phalanx:
those formations hold their shape only at a walk, and an auto-sprint will
break them. Keep Walk advance on whenever you are using a tight formation that
would crumble if the unit broke into a run.

The AUTO sprint-override also matters for charge timing: a unit that enters
sprint range will accelerate through any missile fire it still faces --- gaining
maximum speed at contact rather than jogging through the kill zone. Use that to
your advantage when committing cavalry or an assault infantry charge.

::: {#fig-pace-modes .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/pace_modes.jpg">
  <source src="media/pace_modes.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Pace modes: regiments walk on approach, jog when enemy archers open fire,
then sprint at full charge speed within ~200 px of the target &mdash; all three
AUTO phases in one advance. Toggle Walk advance (☰ Menu) to lock units to
walk pace throughout.
:::

### Choosing a gait by click count

AUTO pace is the default, but you can lock a move order to a specific gait
instead: right-click a destination once for **Walk**, twice for **Jog**,
three times for **Run**, and four or more times for **Sprint** --- click the
same spot again within the multi-click window to add another click to the
count. A click on a new destination (or one far enough from the last click,
or one that arrives after the window has passed) restarts the count at Walk.

There are only three real paces per unit type (for cavalry, walk/trot/gallop
--- see the speed table above). Run has no pace of its own: it holds jog
speed while still far from the destination and switches to sprint speed
once inside the same close-range threshold AUTO uses, so it's a distance-gated
mix of the two rather than a fourth gait.

This overrides AUTO for that order: an explicit gait holds for the whole
move, ignoring the fire/distance triggers AUTO would otherwise use. Reach
for a chosen gait when you want a unit to hold a slow, orderly walk past a
threat it would otherwise jog or sprint through (screening a flank, keeping
a formation intact under fire without also toggling Walk advance), or to
force an early sprint before AUTO's close-range threshold would normally
trigger it.

::: {#fig-multi-click-speeds .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/multi_click_speeds.jpg">
  <source src="media/multi_click_speeds.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Four identical Cavalry regiments sent on the same move, one click apart:
Walk, Jog, Run, and Sprint pull apart into four distinct paces instead of
moving in lockstep.
:::

## Side-stepping the line

A small lateral move order is carried out as a **side-step**: the unit shuffles
sideways while holding its facing, instead of pivoting to face the move and
pivoting back. That matters when you are dressing a line just before contact.
Pivoting a regiment turns it away from the enemy for the length of the turn ---
its front momentarily points sideways, and the ranks lose their dressing while
the soldiers reshuffle. A side-step keeps every soldier looking at the enemy and
keeps the line aligned, so you can nudge a unit a few paces to close a gap or
overlap an enemy flank without ever presenting your own flank in the process.

The cut-off is roughly a unit's own width: a move that heads forward, wider than
that or not, turns and marches normally. A **wider lateral shift** is a different
maneuver again --- see the lateral pivot below --- rather than a plain centre
pivot. So to reposition laterally in good order, make the adjustment small --- a
short step keeps the line intact, a long one commits the unit to a pivot and a
march.

## The lateral pivot

A move too far to the side to side-step, but not far enough off-facing to count
as a move to the rear, is a **file march**: the regiment **quarter-turns**
toward the destination's side in place, marches straight there **keeping its
pre-turn footprint** --- the block never reforms into a line facing the new
heading, so it stays exactly the shape it started as, moving sideways relative
to its own printed grid --- and then **quarter-turns back** to its original
facing once it arrives. The unit ends up facing precisely the way it started;
the reorientation only ever served the march itself.

Relative to the *new* heading, this reads as ranks and files trading places for
the length of the march: what was the frontage (spread across the line) becomes
the marching column's depth, and what was the depth becomes its narrow
frontage --- soldiers advance in file, the way a column on the road does, not
shoulder to shoulder. From the outside the block's world silhouette doesn't
visibly rotate at all while it travels --- only the individual soldiers' own
facing, and the direction they're headed, change. Because nothing ever
reforms, a lateral pivot has no countermarch cost the way a move to the rear's
reform does --- a depleted unit executes it exactly as fast as a fresh one.

::: {#fig-lateral-pivot-flank-march .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/lateral_pivot_flank_march.jpg">
  <source src="media/lateral_pivot_flank_march.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

A Spearmen line ordered far to its own right: it quarter-turns toward the
destination in place, marches straight there without ever reforming (the wide
line stays wide, sliding sideways relative to its own shape), then
quarter-turns back to its original facing on arrival.
:::

## Backing up costs time

An in-place maneuver that makes men *back up* takes longer than one where they
step forward. When a formation **reshapes** without the block marching --- a
frontage change, a centre pivot on the spot --- some soldiers have to move
backward relative to the way they face to reach their new slots. Troops shuffle
backward at only a fraction of their forward (jog) pace, so those men lag, and
the whole maneuver settles more slowly than it would if everyone were stepping
forward. That fraction varies by type --- a heavily-kitted Spearman shuffles
backward at barely a third of his jog pace, while a lighter Archer is closer to
half --- so a reshape's settling time depends on which type is doing the
backing up, not just how far it must move. The practical upshot: a reshape that
pushes the rear ranks backward (deepening the line) is the slowest to dress,
and slowest of all for the heaviest-kitted troops. Order it with a margin of
time before contact, not at the moment of impact.

::: {#fig-back-speed-by-type .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/back_speed_by_type.jpg">
  <source src="media/back_speed_by_type.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Per-type backward-walk speed: a Spearmen and an Archers regiment, forced to the
same formation density and given the same rear-move order at the same tick.
The lighter-kitted Archers settle their countermarch and step off first; the
heavier-kitted Spearmen are still holding position when the clip ends.
:::

The **about-face (conversio)** and **quarter-turn** are not reshapes, so this
penalty doesn't touch them: every soldier turns in place and holds their exact
position for the whole turn --- nobody steps forward or backward at all, only
facing changes. Their duration depends solely on the drill turn rate, not the
backward-speed cap.

The **reform after an about-face** is where the penalty comes back. Flipping a
unit that has taken losses puts its short, under-strength rank --- previously the
safe rear rank --- at the new front, so a rear move reforms after the turn: the
men counter-march through the block onto ranks squared to the new heading,
bringing a full rank forward and sending the short rank back. Some of those men
are stepping backward relative to their facing, so a deep, depleted block takes a
couple of seconds to dress. With **Reform before move** on, that time is spent
before the unit steps off --- order the retreat with a margin. With it off, the
unit marches at once and reforms on arrival, buying speed at the price of leading
the whole march with its thinnest rank.

This same file-countermarch mechanism is also available as its own standalone
drill --- see [How to play: Controls](how-to-play.qmd#controls) for the
Countermarch (exelismos) hotkeys.
A rear-move order reforms *incidentally*, as a side effect of turning to march
somewhere; the standalone drill runs it *deliberately*, always bringing a full
rank forward, and offers a choice of where the whole regiment ends up
afterward: the same ground (Choral/Persian), advanced (Macedonian), or
withdrawn (Laconian).
Use it instead of a rear-move order when you want the full-rank reform without
also committing to a specific destination.

## The centre pivot

A larger turn is a **centre pivot**: the unit rotates about its own centre onto its
new heading in good order rather than snapping round. When the turn is sharp the unit
pivots before it advances --- it nearly halts, faces its destination, then marches ---
so it never slides sideways or backwards at speed. A destination to the **rear**
therefore triggers a full 180° pivot: the whole regiment turns around to face where it
is going before it steps off. (Don't confuse that with the **conversio** about-face on
<kbd>V</kbd>, where each soldier turns in place and the block does not rotate.)

The cost is time: a sharp reorientation spends a beat pivoting before the unit
makes ground, and a 180° pivot spends the most. Plan turns ahead of contact ---
a unit told to reverse at the moment of impact will spend its pivot facing the
wrong way. Note that a **chasing or charging** unit turns instantly instead, so an
attack order never pays the pivot tax on the *approach*; the orderly centre pivot is
reserved for your own move orders, where keeping the ranks dressed is worth the beat.

**Re-facing to fight** has a cost of its own. When a unit engages an enemy that is
roughly a quarter-turn or more off its current front --- attacked in the flank, or
sent to hit a foe off to one side --- it does **not** snap its facing round.
Instead the whole line **turns in place** to bring its front to bear, the men
holding their ground as the block pivots, and it withholds its blows until it
has come about. A small correction still snaps and fights at once, so a unit
already roughly fronting its target loses no time. The upshot: catching a unit on
the flank buys you a genuine window --- it must turn to answer you before it can
strike back --- so a flank attack rewards you both with the flank casualty bonus
*and* the beat the enemy spends coming about. Once the turn settles, the unit
by default just fights with whatever edge it now presents; the engine also
supports reshaping the frontage right after the turn --- back to the width it
had before turning, or to match the frontage of the enemy it turned to face
--- reusing the same width-resize easing as a manual frontage drag, so the
reshape never teleports the ranks. There's no in-game control to pick a
non-default reshape yet, so every unit still fights this out today.

The pivot itself also **slows as the unit picks up speed** --- a unit turns
fastest from a stand and tightens its turn rate the faster it's already moving
(down to 40% of the stationary rate at a full sprint), reflecting the real
lateral force a moving body or formation can exert without losing footing or
cohesion. So a turn ordered from a standstill snaps around quickest; one
ordered mid-charge takes noticeably longer to come about.

Speed itself isn't instant either: a unit **ramps up** to its target pace
(walk, jog, or sprint) and **ramps down** when slowing or stopping, rather than
snapping to a new speed. Heavier-kitted troops (spearmen) build up speed
slower than lighter ones (archers), and every foot type slows down faster than
it speeds up --- stopping takes no effort, building momentum does. Cavalry is
symmetric: a galloping horse can't be reined in any faster than it can build
up speed in the first place.

Arrival works the same way: a unit closing on its ordered point **brakes over
the final stretch** and comes to rest on the marker, instead of charging to the
wire and lurching to a halt past it. Only the final stop brakes --- a queued
waypoint route flows through its corners at pace, and a **charge never slows**:
a unit closing on an enemy carries full speed into contact, so braking costs
you nothing on the attack.

::: {#fig-decel-arrival .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/decel_arrival.jpg">
  <source src="media/decel_arrival.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Braking on arrival: a lone Infantry regiment sprints toward a move order,
eases off over the last stretch, and settles exactly on the ordered point &mdash;
no lurching stop, no coasting past the marker.
:::

The same friction applies even when a unit loses its momentum **without** a
deliberate stop --- its last target dies with no new order queued, say. Its
speed doesn't just tick down to zero on the spot while the unit sits frozen:
it keeps sliding forward along the direction it was actually heading, slowing
under the same braking rate, until it drifts to a genuine halt. Watch a
cavalry regiment that wipes out its target mid-charge: it carries real charge
speed into that final moment, so it visibly coasts on for a beat before
coming to rest, instead of stopping dead the instant it has nothing left to
chase.

::: {#fig-coast-to-stop .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/coast_to_stop.jpg">
  <source src="media/coast_to_stop.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Coasting to a stop: a lone Cavalry regiment wipes out its target mid-charge;
with no enemy left to chase and no new order, it keeps sliding forward on its
residual charge speed, decelerating, before finally coming to rest.
:::

## Wheeling on a flank

A **wheel** (<kbd>Z</kbd> left / <kbd>C</kbd> right) is the third way to change a line's
front, and the most useful in the line of battle. One end --- the standing flank file ---
plants and holds while the rest of the block swings around it like a door on a hinge. The
line ends up facing 90° across from where it started *and* lying along a new bearing,
with every man still in his rank and file.

It sits between the two in-place turns and the centre pivot:

- The **quarter-turn** (<kbd>Q</kbd> / <kbd>E</kbd>) changes *facing* but not *place* ---
  the men turn where they stand and the block stays put.
- The **centre pivot** (a move order) rotates the whole block about its middle, so both
  flanks sweep and the line stays centred on the same spot.
- The **wheel** pins one flank and sweeps the other, so the line **hinges** rather than
  spinning in place --- the near flank barely moves while the far flank marches a wide arc.

That hinge is what makes it a battle-line maneuver. Wheel a wing forward to take an enemy
line in the **flank** --- the ×1.5 side bonus (or ×2 in the rear) is the whole point of
getting around them. Wheel a wing back to **refuse a flank** when the enemy laps around
your end, presenting a fresh front instead of an exposed side. Because the far flank has
to march an arc, a wheel takes time and covers ground: order it before contact, not in the
grinding melee, and give the swinging flank room to travel. The swing is paced by the
**outer file** --- the corner man farthest from the hinge jogs his arc, and every inner
file slows proportionally to hold the line straight --- so a wide or deep block wheels
noticeably slower than a compact one. A fighting unit ignores the
order --- you can't hinge a line that's locked in combat.

The same hinge swing also fires **automatically** as part of a large oblique move to the
rear: if a rear-sector destination is oblique enough that the about-face's own 180°
reversal alone would still leave a sizeable turn to close, the regiment about-faces, THEN
wheels the rest of the way onto the destination's bearing, THEN marches --- so a unit
sent far to the rear on an angle steps off already lined up on its destination instead of
correcting the last of the turn mid-march. You don't order this one; it's the drilled
default for that shape of move (see [how-to-play](how-to-play.qmd#fig-wheel-turn)).

## Order stances

Each order can carry a *stance*, armed with a hotkey before you click (a coloured
cursor shows which is active; <kbd>Esc</kbd> clears it). Stances turn the same units
into different tools:

- **Hold** (<kbd>H</kbd>) --- the unit stands its ground instead of chasing bait.
  Anchor a line, or keep a screen in place while your cavalry swings wide.
- **Attack flank** (<kbd>F</kbd>) / **Attack rear** (<kbd>R</kbd>) --- the unit
  maneuvers for the side or back rather than the front, cashing in the ×1.5 / ×2
  multipliers above. Pair it with a frontal pin.
- **Skirmish** (<kbd>K</kbd>) --- a ranged unit kites: it backs off when a threat
  closes inside its firing range, trading ground to keep shooting rather than being
  caught in melee.
- **Support** (<kbd>G</kbd>) --- the unit guards a chosen ally, shadowing it and
  intercepting any enemy that closes on it. Good for gluing spearmen to vulnerable
  horse, or a guard onto your archers.
- **Cycle charge** (<kbd>J</kbd>) --- a caracole for cavalry: the unit charges,
  lands its impact hit, then peels back to a standoff and charges again, instead of
  grinding in a static melee where the charge bonus is already spent. The charge
  bonus scales with closing speed, so each fresh run lands hard --- but don't cycle
  into braced spears, where a fast charge backfires.
- **Sweep routers** (<kbd>,</kbd>) --- the unit prioritizes a routing (fleeing) enemy
  over a closer enemy that's still standing and fighting, even as the gap to the
  router narrows. Arm it on a unit you want cleaning up broken formations rather
  than getting drawn into (or staying stuck in) a fresh melee with whoever happens
  to be nearest --- a natural pairing with a flanking cavalry sweep once the enemy
  line starts to crack.
- **Roll the line** (<kbd>;</kbd>) --- once the current target routs or is
  destroyed, the unit automatically re-targets the next-closest enemy still
  fighting instead of standing idle or chasing a broken foe down. A single
  attack order can carry a unit through a whole line of weaker enemies one
  after another --- useful once you've broken through a flank and want the
  advance to keep rolling without a fresh order for every foe.
- **Pin down** (<kbd>.</kbd>) --- a stall/pin trade for a unit confronted head-on: it
  swings roughly half as often as a normal stance, so it deals less damage over
  time --- but each swing's follow-through opens a brief window where its own
  defense is lowered, so a foe that catches it mid-swing hits harder than it
  otherwise would. Use it to hold a line cheaply in casualties from its own
  attacks, not to win a stand-up fight outright; it's a genuine tradeoff, not a
  strict upgrade over a normal stance.
- **All-out attack** (<kbd>'</kbd>) --- a straight offense/defense trade: +20%
  effective attack, but the unit's own defense drops 20% while it's fighting
  all-out. It hits harder and dies faster at the same time, so use it to force
  a quick, costly decision --- finishing off a wavering enemy, or a rearguard
  buying time --- not as a strict upgrade over a normal stance.
- **Chase** (<kbd>\\</kbd>) --- relentless pursuit: the unit overrides the normal
  disengage exit a plain move order (or losing steady contact) otherwise gives
  it, so it keeps closing on and re-engaging the same fleeing or repositioning
  enemy instead of breaking off. Use it to run down a routing foe rather than
  let it escape to rally and rejoin the fight later.
- **Wedge charge** (<kbd>-</kbd>) --- maximizes the approach at the cost of
  defense and stamina: a faster pace toward the target, a lower own defense
  while fighting, and faster fatigue buildup while engaged. Use it to punch a
  hole in the enemy line before it can brace, not as a strict upgrade over a
  normal stance --- the unit that arrives first is also the easier one to hit.
- **Knockback focus** (<kbd>=</kbd>) --- an offense-only trade: the unit hits
  40% softer, but every blow shoves the enemy back much harder and is more
  likely to fell it outright. Hold <kbd>Shift</kbd> when you arm or issue it
  to pick the push distance: the plain hotkey shoves a struck enemy just far
  enough to clear the battle line, while Shift+hotkey drives it much further
  back. Use it to break up a packed formation or buy room to maneuver, not to
  win a straight fight --- the softer hits mean it trades its own damage
  output for control of space.

A volley is not instant: the arrows fly a real arc and **land where they were aimed
a moment later**, so a fast unit can partly outrun a shot loosed at long range, and
casualties arrive on the beat the arrows come down rather than the instant they leave
the bow.

## Group attack distribution

When you have several units selected and right-click a target, press <kbd>X</kbd> to
cycle between two group-order modes. The same toggle governs both an attack on an
enemy and a relief order on a friendly (see [Passage of lines](#passage-of-lines)) ---
which pair of behaviours applies depends only on what you right-click:

- **Focused** (default) --- every selected unit charges the same enemy, or (on a
  friendly target) every reliever swaps with the one tired unit clicked.
  Use it to overwhelm a single regiment quickly.
- **Distributed** --- each unit picks a different nearby enemy, sorted by proximity
  to the clicked target; on a friendly target, each reliever instead pairs with its
  own nearby engaged ally the same way. Extra units cycle through the list if you
  have more units than targets. Use it to hold an entire line rather than piling
  onto one regiment (or one relief swap) and leaving the rest unengaged.

The flash message in the top-centre of the screen confirms the active mode. The mode
is sticky --- it stays set until you press <kbd>X</kbd> again (<kbd>Shift</kbd> + <kbd>X</kbd>
cycles the other way).

::: {#fig-group-attack .demo}

```{=html}
<video class="demo-video" controls muted loop playsinline preload="metadata"
       poster="media/group_attack.jpg">
  <source src="media/group_attack.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  <a href="https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta">repository</a> to run the game.
</video>
```

Group attack distribution: three regiments in distributed mode each target
a different nearby enemy instead of all piling onto one.
:::

## Terrain

Two types of terrain patch appear on the battle field:

::: {#tbl-terrain}

| Patch | Colour | Effect |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Forest** | Dark green | Passable - units can enter, but movement is slowed to **60%** of normal speed |
| **Hill** | Tan | **Impassable** - units route around it via A\* pathfinding |

Terrain patches and their movement effects.
:::

Units automatically path around impassable terrain when ordered to move. The forest
is a different trade-off: going *around* it costs distance; going *through* it costs
time. Generally it is faster to push cavalry or fast-moving infantry through rather
than around a narrow forest.

A [routing](#morale-routing) unit paths around a hill the same way while fleeing ---
it's no longer a straight sprint toward the back edge. If a router is boxed in with
every escape direction blocked, it gives up fleeing and stands to fight rather than
running in place against the obstacle.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Forests slow cavalry charges

A cavalry unit that contacts the enemy while still inside a forest hits with a reduced
charge bonus --- it can't build full speed in the trees. Stage your charges from open
ground whenever possible.
:::

## Order-response delay

Units don't react instantly to orders. There is a brief **reaction delay** before they
execute --- about **0.5 s** for infantry and spearmen, **0.3 s** for cavalry. A unit
already in melee is not slowed by the delay.

Drill commands (the wheel, about-face, quarter-turn, and countermarch) carry their own,
shorter **drill response beat** --- about **0.2 s** by default between the command and
the men starting the evolution. A drill is a rehearsed, single-evolution response to a
direct order, so the beat is quicker than planning a fresh march. It is a per-unit
characteristic: an elite regiment can be staged with a snappier beat and a raw levy
with a sluggish one, so the same command visibly ripples through a mixed line.

This models the time for orders to travel down the chain of command. In practice:

- **Commit cavalry early.** If you wait until the last moment to redirect horse, they
  may not reach the flank in time.
- **Feints are risky.** A unit you pull away from melee takes rear hits during the
  disengage *and* takes a moment to start moving --- don't disengage into a second
  threat without enough margin.

## Disengaging --- a risky lever

Right-click the ground (a **move** order) to pull a unit *out* of melee. It's how
you retreat a battered regiment or redeploy cavalry to a fresh flank.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Disengaging exposes your back
While a unit marches away it shows its **rear** to whatever it left --- so that enemy
gets free **rear hits (×2)** until your unit is clear. Pull out at the right moment,
or pull out into a disaster.
:::

Stop the unit and it re-engages anything nearby. Timing the disengage --- late enough
that the enemy is committed, early enough that you survive the exit --- is one of the
game's sharper decisions.

---

*Source: [`README.md`](https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta/blob/main/README.md) ·
combat math lives in
[`scripts/Unit.gd`](https://github.com/Lacaedemon/sparta/blob/main/scripts/Unit.gd)
(`_strike()`, `_flank_multiplier()`).*
